stories.title: The Recycling Project
stories.story:
When our school announced a new recycling initiative, most students groaned. It meant extra work: sorting paper, plastic, and cans into separate bins every single week. I'll admit, I wasn't excited about it either.

Our teacher, Mr. Fahmy, asked our class to research what actually happens to waste that isn't recycled. What we found surprised almost everyone. Plastic bottles could take hundreds of years to break down completely. Entire mountains of trash were piling up in places most of us had never even thought about.

Suddenly, the project felt less like a chore and more like something that mattered. I started noticing how much plastic my own family used in a single week, and it was honestly a bit shocking. I began separating our recycling at home without anyone even asking me to.

After three months, our school announced the results: we had collected enough recyclable material to fill an entire truck. Mr. Fahmy told us that small actions, repeated by many people, could genuinely add up to something significant. I still don't love sorting the bins every week, but now I understand why it's worth doing, and that understanding has made all the difference.

stories.title: The Big Decision
stories.story:
Sign-ups for after-school clubs were due by Friday, and Karim couldn't decide between two very different options: the debate club, which met on Tuesdays, or the football team, which practiced on the exact same afternoons. He loved arguing his opinions just as much as he loved scoring goals, and choosing between them felt impossible.

He asked his older sister for advice. "Just pick the one you're better at," she suggested. But Karim wasn't sure that was the right way to decide something this important. He wasn't choosing based on skill — he was choosing based on what he actually wanted his afternoons to feel like.

Instead of rushing the decision, Karim visited both clubs as a guest before signing up for either. At debate club, he found himself thinking deeply about topics he'd never considered before, and he genuinely enjoyed the challenge of defending an argument. At football practice, he simply had fun, without needing to overthink anything at all.

In the end, Karim chose debate club, not because it was easier, but because it made him curious about the world in a way football didn't. He still played football with friends on weekends, just without the pressure of a team schedule. He realized that a good decision wasn't always about picking the option everyone expected — it was about understanding what actually mattered to him.
